sources.exact_document_title: DHA Telehealth Clinical Guidelines for Virtual Management of Atopic Dermatitis
sources.population: Children and adults with atopic dermatitis complaints.
sources.clinical_setting: Telehealth services in DHA-licensed health facilities.
sources.applicability_note: Direct for Dubai telehealth atopic-dermatitis documentation; partial for every eczema or contact-dermatitis subtype.

exact_sections.heading: 5.1 Virtual Clinical Assessment; Common Features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Covers itch, dry skin, erythema, vesicles, crusting, scaling, excoriation, lichenification, fissuring, age-linked distribution, atopy, sleep, and quality-of-life impact.

exact_sections.heading: 7. Red Flags
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists non-blanching rash, rapidly worsening painful eczema, fever or lethargy, respiratory distress, clustered blisters, punched-out erosions, recurrent infection, pregnancy, and psychosocial concerns.

exact_sections.heading: 8. Diagnosis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports morphology and distribution review, atopy history, dry skin, selected non-routine tests, and differential-diagnosis assessment.

exact_sections.heading: 10. Management
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Covers clinician-led trigger reduction, skin-barrier care, education, and pharmacological choices; no medicine dose is mapped.

sources.exact_document_title: DHA Telehealth Clinical Guidelines for Virtual Management of Rashes in Children – 49
sources.clinical_setting: Telehealth services in DHA-licensed health facilities.
sources.applicability_note: Direct for pediatric-rash telehealth documentation in Dubai; medication and dose passages were reviewed but deliberately not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: 2. Scope; 3. Purpose; 4. Applicability
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines pediatric rash use in DHA-licensed telehealth and excludes emergency and controlled-medication scenarios.

exact_sections.heading: 5. Causes
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Organises pediatric rashes by fever, itching, and neither fever nor itching without proving a diagnosis for an individual child.

exact_sections.heading: 6. Assessment and history
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies visual assessment plus duration, evolution, distribution, symptoms, family history, recurrence, exposures, medication, impact, travel, allergy, and pet history.

exact_sections.heading: 10. Referral Criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists emergency and face-to-face review criteria, including confusion, stiff neck, photophobia, persistent or recurrent rash, fever, immunocompromise, and diagnostic uncertainty.

sources.exact_document_title: Emergency treatment of anaphylaxis: Guidelines for healthcare providers
sources.population: Adults and children with suspected anaphylaxis.
sources.applicability_note: Used only to support anaphylaxis warning documentation associated with rash or urticaria; it does not convert isolated skin findings into anaphylaxis.

exact_sections.heading: Key recommendations; 2.1 Definition of anaphylaxis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

exact_sections.heading: Key recommendations and trigger discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting known allergen exposure, food or drug triggers, action-plan discussion, and specialist follow-up after suspected anaphylaxis.
